reviews.title: The Sensemaking Process and Leverage Points for Analyst Technology
reviews.summary: A cognitive task analysis account of intelligence analysis as a two-loop sensemaking process, identifying tool leverage points for evidence foraging, schema construction, hypothesis management, and bias control.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Learning Loop Complex theory of sensemaking.
Figure 2. Notional model of the sensemaking loop for intelligence analysis.
Figures 4 and 5. Analyst-drawn hypothesis sketch and exploration-enrichment-exploitation tradeoff.

reviews.title: xHC: Expanded Hyper-Connections
reviews.summary: xHC is the first Hyper-Connections method to scale residual-stream expansion beyond N=4 in Transformers, using temporal feature augmentation and sparse residual updates to achieve meaningful gains at N=16 with only modest FLOPs overhead.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Expansion efficiency: loss vs. FLOPs on a 2.5B MoE model.
Figure 2a. Training loss comparison at 18B MoE scale.
Figure 2b. Average downstream score comparison.

tables.caption: Table 1. Downstream evaluation on 18B and 28B MoE models.
Table 2. Ablation study on xHC components.
Table 4. Per-token memory access analysis.

reviews.title: OvisOCR2 Technical Report
reviews.summary: OvisOCR2 is a 0.8B end-to-end document parsing model achieving SOTA on OmniDocBench v1.6 (96.58) and PureDocBench (75.06 Avg3), surpassing all pipeline methods through a two-branch training recipe of SFT, RL with multi-component rewards, on-policy distillation, and model fusion.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Performance of OvisOCR2 on OmniDocBench v1.6 — the highest overall score among all methods.
Figure 4. Training stability comparison between 4B RL and 0.8B RL — direct 0.8B RL shows higher KL drift and lower table quality.
Figure 3. Two-branch training architecture — 4B branch produces RL-aligned teacher, 0.8B branch proceeds through SFT, OPD, and model fusion.

reviews.title: Ring-Zero: Scaling Zero RL to a Trillion Parameters for Emergent Reasoning
reviews.summary: Scales zero RL to a 1T-parameter MoE model, demonstrating that at sufficient scale the model spontaneously develops advanced cognitive behaviors (anthropomorphism, structured formatting, self-verification, parallel reasoning) without hand-engineered heuristics, validating the bitter lesson for LLM reasoning.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Overview of Ring-2.5-1T-Zero: multi-stage training pipeline, infrastructure optimizations, and emergent behaviors.
Figure 2. Training curves of Ling-2.5-1T-Base during first stage RL showing reward and sequence length growth.
Figure 3. Evaluation of CoT quality across comprehensibility, reproducibility, and efficiency dimensions.

tables.caption: Table 1. Main results on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (pass@1 accuracy %).
Table 2. Stage-specific hyperparameter configurations.

reviews.title: Remember When It Matters: Proactive Memory Agent for Long-Horizon Agents
reviews.summary: A separate memory agent maintains a structured memory bank and selectively injects grounded reminders into an action agent's context, framing memory as an active intervention policy. Gains of +8.3 pp on Terminal-Bench and +6.8 pp on τ²-Bench, with open-weight SFT+GRPO training showing partial transfer.

figures.caption: Figure 1. System integration. The memory agent (right) runs as a separate process beside the action agent (left), observing a sliding window of recent steps and the current memory store.
Figure 2. Memory agent internals. Phase 1 manages the structured memory bank through tool calls. Phase 2 reads the updated bank and emits either a context reminder or no intervention.

tables.caption: Table 1. Main results on Terminal-Bench and τ²-Bench. All scores are pass@1 percentages.
Table 2. Ablations on τ²-Bench with Sonnet 4.5 as the action agent, Opus 4.6 as the memory agent.
Table 4. Training and transfer of the open-weight memory agent. SETA validation and Terminal-Bench transfer results.

reviews.title: UI-MOPD: Multi-Platform On-Policy Distillation for Continual GUI Agent Learning
reviews.summary: UI-MOPD trains a single 8B GUI agent for cross-platform (desktop + mobile) interaction using platform-conditioned on-policy KL distillation from 32B expert teachers, achieving 38.2% on OSWorld and 12.0% on MobileWorld — outperforming mixed SFT and model merging baselines while preserving general GUI grounding.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Motivation of UI-MOPD: naive mixing of desktop and mobile signals produces an averaged policy; UI-MOPD uses platform-conditioned routing and multi-teacher on-policy distillation.
Figure 2. Overview of UI-MOPD training pipeline with platform-specific teachers and platform-conditioned distillation.
Figure 6. Mobile task execution example of UI-MOPD.

reviews.title: LLM-as-a-Tutor: Policy-Aware Prompt Adaptation for Non-Verifiable RL
reviews.summary: LLM-as-a-Tutor extends the LLM's role from judge to tutor in non-verifiable RL, appending atomic constraints to non-discriminative prompts to restore reward signal variance. The append-only design monotonically raises difficulty with the policy's capability, outperforming rubric-only and policy-unaware baselines.

figures.caption: Figure 1. LLM-as-a-Tutor concept: the tutor examines policy rollouts and appends atomic constraints to non-discriminative prompts.
Figure 2a. Constraint-added rate across policy scales.
Figure 2b. Base-rubric reward according to the number of appended constraints.

tables.caption: Table 1. Main results on instruction-following benchmarks.
Table 2. Ablation on policy-adaptiveness and prompt modification strategies.
Table 3. Additional ablation and scaling results.

reviews.title: Orca: The World is in Your Mind
reviews.summary: Orca is a general world foundation model that learns a unified world latent space from vision and language via Next-State-Prediction, using unconscious learning (dense video state transitions) and conscious learning (event-conditioned and VQA supervision); the frozen backbone supports downstream text, image, and action readouts, outperforming similar-sized specialized baselines.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Orca's overall Encoder-Decoder framework.
Figure 2. Encoder overview with unconscious and conscious learning paradigms.

tables.caption: Table 1. Text generation comparison across 4 benchmarks.
Table 2. Cross-benchmark capability comparison.
Table 3. Image prediction comparison on PRICE-V0.1 benchmark.

reviews.title: Can LLM-as-a-Judge Reliably Verify Rubrics in Agentic Scenarios?
reviews.summary: RuVerBench is the first benchmark for assessing LLM-as-a-Judge reliability in rubric verification for agentic scenarios, covering Deep Research and Agentic Coding with 2,458 human-annotated instances; even frontier models exhibit substantial noise, with weaker models more sensitive to prompt variations, batching trading accuracy for efficiency, and majority voting yielding diminishing returns.

figures.caption: Figure 1. LLM-as-a-Judge rubric verification in agentic scenarios.
Figure 2. RuVerBench construction from existing agentic evaluation datasets.
Figure 3. Dataset provenance and benchmark construction pipeline.

tables.caption: Table 1. RuVerBench taxonomy used for category-level analysis.
Table 2. Main RuVerBench leaderboard.
Table 3. Prompt sensitivity results.

reviews.title: Agentic Abstention: Do Agents Know When to Stop Instead of Act?
reviews.summary: Defines Agentic Abstention as the sequential decision problem of when tool-using LLM agents should stop acting under uncertainty. Across web shopping, terminal, and QA scenarios (28K+ tasks, 13 models, 2 scaffolds), agents struggle primarily with timely abstention — stopping at the earliest warranted step rather than after unnecessary interaction. CONVOLVE, a context-engineering method distilling trajectories into reusable stopping rules, raises Llama-3.3-70B timely recall from 26.7% to 57.4% on WebShop without parameter updates.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Environment-based Abstention example in web shopping, showing timely success, delayed success, and failure to abstain trajectories.
Figure 2. Abstention Recall @K across web, terminal, and QA scenarios — early abstention remains low across settings.
Figure 3. AbsRec@K by abstention category in Web, Terminal, and QA scenarios — Missing Target and Underspecified Intent are hardest.
Figure 4. Reasoning improves AbsRec@1 but decreases overall AbsRec@10 on both Web and Terminal scenarios.
Figure 5. TerminalBench adaptation showing example rewritten instructions for False Premise and Underspecified Intent.

reviews.title: Open Problem: Is AdamW Effective Under Heavy-Tailed Noise?
reviews.summary: A theory note posing the open problem of whether AdamW can match sign-based optimizers under finite-moment heavy-tailed gradient noise, with a positive weighted benchmark and denominator-memory barrier mechanisms.

reviews.title: Unlimited OCR Works
reviews.summary: Reference Sliding Window Attention keeps OCR reference tokens globally visible while bounding generated-token attention and KV-cache growth, enabling one-pass parsing of dozens of pages.

figures.caption: Figure 1. R-SWA keeps all reference tokens visible while restricting generated-token attention to a fixed recent window.
Figure 2. Unlimited OCR combines DeepEncoder with an R-SWA MoE decoder and a fixed-capacity output KV queue.
Figure 3. FlashAttention v3 per-call latency grows for dense attention but remains stable for R-SWA.

tables.caption: Table 1. OmniDocBench v1.5 and v1.6 results across text, formula, table, and reading-order metrics.
Table 2. Text and reading-order edit distance by document category.
Tables 3 and 4. Long-horizon OCR quality by page count and throughput by output length.

reviews.title: AI Engram: In Search of Memory Traces in Artificial Intelligence
reviews.summary: AI Engram identifies concept-specific memory traces through a closed-form covariance projection, enabling one-shot composition or erasure of learned knowledge across vision models and Llama-3.2-1B unlearning.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Neuroscience criteria for an AI engram: specificity, reactivation, sufficiency, and necessity.
Figure 2. Mechanics of the Engram method: layer covariances are accumulated in one forward pass and used to compute engram weights.
Figure 3. Engrammatic decomposition enables zero-shot synthesis of combinatorial memory states from extracted concept engrams.

tables.caption: Table 1. Translating engram criteria into optimization constraints over observed and intervened states.
Table 2. CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 class-wise unlearning comparison with Tug-of-War, DA, and NMI metrics.
Table 3. Llama-3.2-1B TOFU forget10 evaluation comparing Engram with gradient-based and preference-based unlearning baselines.

reviews.title: MiniMax Sparse Attention
reviews.summary: A GQA-native block-sparse attention design that uses a lightweight learned Index Branch and co-designed GPU kernels to preserve long-context model quality while cutting million-token attention cost.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Overview of MSA with an Index Branch selecting key-value blocks and a Main Branch attending over selected blocks.
Figures 2 and 3. Training dynamics for MSA pretraining and sparse continued pretraining.
Figure 4. Efficiency comparison between dense GQA and MSA across context lengths.

tables.caption: Table 2. Representative evaluation results comparing Full Attention, MSA-PT, and MSA-CPT.

reviews.title: Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents
reviews.summary: A graph-memory framework for LLM agents that replaces static top-k retrieval with active Cue-Tag-Content memory reconstruction over long interaction histories.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Passive retrieval versus active reconstruction in MRAgent.
Figure 2. Example where active reconstruction infers an intermediate temporal cue and recovers the needed evidence.
Figure 4. MRAgent associative memory system and active memory reconstruction loop.

tables.caption: Tables 1-3. LoCoMo results, LongMemEval results, and token/runtime comparison.
Table 4. Memory toolkit operations for controlled traversal.

reviews.title: ArcANE: Do Role-Playing Language Agents Stay in Character at the Right Time?
reviews.summary: A benchmark and training setup for evaluating whether role-playing language agents adapt behavior to character arcs across narrative phases.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Out-of-World ArcANE probe example for Harry Potter.
Figure 2. ArcANE pipeline for Character Arc construction and probe generation.
Figure 4. Arc source-of-effect ablation across DeepSeek, Qwen3, and ArcANE-32B-DPO.

tables.caption: Table 1. Comparison with role-playing language agent evaluation benchmarks.
Table 2. Main validated-slice results across models, context modes, probe categories, and metrics.
Table 3. Dataset statistics for train, validated test, and low-popularity test splits.

reviews.title: Where Do Deep-Research Agents Go Wrong? Span-Level Error Localization in Agent Trajectories
reviews.summary: A benchmark and claim-centric auditing framework for localizing harmful error spans in long deep-research agent trajectories.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Data curation pipeline for TELBench.
Figure 2. Mechanism analysis of annotated TELBench trajectories.
Figure 3. DRIFT claim-centric auditing workflow.

reviews.title: SCOPE: Self-Play via Co-Evolving Policies for Open-Ended Tasks
reviews.summary: SCOPE extends data-free self-play to open-ended tasks by co-evolving a Challenger and Solver with a frozen Judge that writes rubrics from source documents. Gains of +5.4 to +10.4 points on open-ended benchmarks, matching GRPOdata trained on ~9K curated prompts, with transfer to short-form QA.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Overview of SCOPE. The Challenger generates document-grounded tasks, the Solver answers via multi-turn retrieval, and the fixed Judge writes rubrics and grades responses.
Figure 2. Reward design ablation on Qwen3-8B. Quality gates and length penalty each prevent a distinct reward-hacking failure.
Figure 3. The Challenger must co-evolve. Co-evolution keeps tasks near the 0.5 difficulty sweet spot.

tables.caption: Table 2. Open-ended benchmark results. SCOPE gains +5.4 to +10.4 points across iterations.
Table 3. Short-form QA results. SCOPE surpasses GRPOdata across models.
Table 4. Controlled replay experiment. Gains arise from both retrieval and synthesis.

reviews.title: Revisiting Observation Reduction for Web Agents: Comprehensive Evaluation with a Lightweight Framework
reviews.summary: Minimal Failure Set (MFS) 기반의 경량 평가 프레임워크를 제안하여 observation reduction 방법을 end-to-end 평가 없이도 효과적으로 비교할 수 있게 한다. Coverage는 end-to-end 성공률과 강한 상관관계(Pearson r > 0.9)를 보이며 100배 이상의 평가 속도 향상을 달성한다. 11가지 방법을 종합 비교하고, MFS 데이터로 최적화된 pruning program(GEPA)이 2.2×–3.1× latency 감소를 이루면서도 84–89%의 성공률을 유지함을 보인다.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Key results of our framework on WorkArena L1 (33 tasks). (a) Coverage versus end-to-end success rate. (b) Evaluation time comparison: end-to-end vs coverage. (c) End-to-end success rate vs per-step latency with Qwen3.5-122B-A10B.
Figure 2. Correlation between coverage and end-to-end success rate across HTML reduction methods on WorkArena and WebLinx.
Figure 3. Coverage versus reduction ratio on WorkArena L1 and WebLinx. Left: retrieval-based vs LLM inference-based methods. Right: MFS-based optimization effect.

tables.caption: Table 1. Evaluation time comparison between end-to-end and coverage evaluation.
Table 2. Average wall-clock time (seconds) to reduce a single HTML observation.
Table 3. Coverage drop (%) when ablating a single HTML element type.

reviews.title: GTA: Generating Long-Horizon Tasks for Web Agents at Scale
reviews.summary: A graph-grounded benchmark-generation framework that crawls live websites and uses retrieval-seeded generation plus verification to create scalable multi-hop web-agent tasks with executable trajectories.

figures.caption: Table 2 and Figure 1. Example GTA tasks compared with WebDS, AgentTrek, and NNetNav, plus page-coverage analysis.
Figure 2. Browser Use and AgentOccam performance on GTA-Intra, GTA-Inter, WebVoyager, and Mind2Web.
Figures 8 and 9. Per-website and cross-lingual performance for Browser Use and AgentOccam.

tables.caption: Table 1. Benchmark-property comparison showing GTA combines multi-hop generation, scalability, executable ground truth, dynamic expansion, and multilingual coverage.
Table 2. Representative generated web tasks and answers across GTA and prior datasets.
Table 3. Human evaluation scores for complexity, utility, realism, and overall quality.

reviews.title: Are VLMs Seeing or Just Saying? Uncovering the Illusion of Visual Re-examination
reviews.summary: VisualSwap probes whether VLMs genuinely re-examine visual content during self-reflection by swapping images mid-reasoning; models overwhelmingly fail to detect changes (up to 60% accuracy drop), thinking models are 3x more vulnerable, and self-reflection fails to mobilize visual attention while explicit user instructions restore grounding.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustrative example of visual re-examination. Left: Given image Ia and query Q, the VLM generates reasoning chain Ra including a self-reflective trigger. Top-right: Under standard inference with Ia, the model validates its logic to reach the correct answer A1. Bottom-right: In the VisualSwap condition, Ia is replaced by a visually similar but semantically distinct image Ib post-reflection. Despite explicitly 'saying' it will re-examine the figure, the model fails to detect the visual discrepancy and yields an incorrect answer A2 grounded in the stale context of Ia.

Figure 2. Visual attention score Svis across decoding steps for Qwen3-VL-8B (layers 18-21). Probe shows lower attention than baseline throughout generation. Multi-turn elevates attention substantially after the user instruction.
Figure 3. Visual attention score Svis across decoding steps for Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B (layers 54-57). The same pattern holds at scale: probe suppresses visual attention while multi-turn restores it.

tables.caption: Table 1. Image similarity metrics for pairs (Ia, Ib) in VS-BENCH. Higher CLIP/SSIM and lower LPIPS indicate greater similarity.
Table 2. Main results on VS-BENCH. Base and Probe accuracies alongside the performance drop (Delta) across 15 VLMs.
Table 3. Average baseline accuracy on Ia vs. Ib under standard inference and Table 4. Average accuracy under Probe vs. Multi-turn. Explicit user instruction substantially recovers performance.

reviews.title: G-Zero: Self-Play for Open-Ended Generation from Zero Data
reviews.summary: G-Zero introduces Hint-δ, a verifier-free intrinsic reward for self-evolution in open-ended domains, where a Proposer (GRPO) generates challenging query-hint pairs and a Generator (DPO) internalizes the hint-guided improvements without external judges.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Comparison of self-supervision signals.
Figure 2. G-Zero co-evolutionary loop.
Figure 3. Capability scaling dynamics.
Figure 4. Hint-δ distribution shift across rounds.

tables.caption: Table 1. Main results.
Tables 2 & 3. DPO pool composition and δ-filter design space.
Table 4. Hyperparameters.

reviews.title: Odysseys: Benchmarking Web Agents on Realistic Long Horizon Tasks
reviews.summary: A 200-task long-horizon web agent benchmark derived from real browsing sessions, with rubric-based evaluation achieving higher human agreement than trajectory-level LLM judges; frontier models score only 44.5% perfect success, revealing major room for improvement in long-horizon web navigation.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Odysseys is a long-horizon web agent benchmark with 200 tasks based on real user browsing data.
Figure 2. After researching 10 surgeon profiles, GPT-5.4 encoded the entire data table as a base64 string.
Figure 3. A scatter plot of perfect success rate against number of steps taken.

tables.caption: Table 2. Model performance on Odysseys.

reviews.title: Read More, Think More: Revisiting Observation Reduction for Web Agents
reviews.summary: 웹 에이전트에서 HTML 관측 축소(observation reduction)가 항상 정답이 아님을 보여준다. 고성능 모델은 HTML의 풍부한 레이아웃 정보를 활용해 더 높은 성공률을 달성하고(최대 +17.5%p), thinking token 예산이 많을수록 HTML의 이점이 커지는 반면, 저성능 모델은 긴 입력으로 인한 환각으로 성능이 저하된다.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Relationship between observation representation and task success rate (WorkArena L1). The x-axis shows the average number of input tokens per step; the y-axis shows the task success rate.
Figure 2. Grounding error counts and their breakdown for a11y and HTML across models (WorkArena L1).

Figure 3. Relationship between action repetition rate and task success rate (WorkArena L1). The x-axis shows the rate of actions identical to the previous step; the y-axis shows the task success rate.

tables.caption: Table 1. Task success rate (%) by observation representation and model, evaluated on WorkArena L1.
Table 2. Task success rate (%) by observation format (WorkArena L1). Comparing XML format vs indent format for both a11y and HTML representations.
Table 3. Impact of switching from a11y to HTML on task success rate by category (WorkArena L1).

reviews.title: How to Steal Reasoning Without Reasoning Traces
reviews.summary: A trace-inversion attack showing that hidden chain-of-thought traces can be approximated from answers and summaries well enough to fine-tune student models and transfer reasoning capability.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Example of Trace Inversion against GPT-5.4 mini from query, summary, and final answer to synthesized trace.
Figure 2. Trace Inversion pipeline: train on surrogate traces, invert victim outputs, then fine-tune a student on synthetic traces.
Figure 3. Student accuracy as a function of victim-query budget.

tables.caption: Table 1. Feature distribution of surrogate summaries versus GPT-5.4 mini summaries.
Table 2. Trace inversion fidelity against the open-weight R1 victim.
Table 3. Downstream accuracy after fine-tuning when attacking R1.

reviews.title: Less is Enough: Synthesizing Diverse Data in LLM Feature Space with Sparse Autoencoders
reviews.summary: A coverage-guided data synthesis framework (FAC Synthesis) that uses SAE-based feature activation coverage to identify missing task-relevant features in LLM post-training data and generate targeted synthetic samples, achieving SOTA on four tasks with 150x fewer samples than MAGPIE.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Efficiency frontier of instruction following datasets
Figure 2. FAC Synthesis: a coverage-guided synthetic framework
Figure 3. The results of the relationship between FAC and AUPRC

reviews.summary: A reasoning-first process reward model and WebPRMBench benchmark for ranking web-agent actions with principle-guided justifications and stronger reward-guided trajectory search.

figures.caption: Figure 1. WebPRMBench performance comparison across model scale and environments.

reviews.title: Web World Models
reviews.summary: Web World Models use ordinary web code for deterministic world state and rules while LLMs generate typed narrative and contextual content, yielding persistent open-ended environments with stronger controllability than fully generative worlds.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Web World Models are positioned between fixed database-backed web frameworks and fully generative world models.
Figure 3. WWM architecture separates deterministic code-defined physics from stochastic LLM-generated imagination.
Figure 5. Infinite Travel Atlas interaction flow from coordinate selection to generated itinerary presentation.

reviews.title: NL2Repo-Bench: Towards Long-Horizon Repository Generation Evaluation of Coding Agents
reviews.summary: NL2Repo-Bench evaluates long-horizon coding agents by asking them to generate complete Python repositories from natural-language specifications and validating outputs with upstream pytest suites.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Example NL2Repo task document showing project overview, environment configuration, project structure, API guide, and implementation nodes.

reviews.title: Prompting Science Report 4: Playing Pretend: Expert Personas Don't Improve Factual Accuracy
reviews.summary: Expert personas do not improve factual accuracy on difficult multiple-choice questions (GPQA Diamond, MMLU-Pro) across six models. Low-knowledge personas (toddler, layperson) often degrade performance. The exception is Gemini 2.0 Flash on MMLU-Pro, showing modest gains from expert personas.

reviews.title: Prune4Web: DOM Tree Pruning Programming for Web Agent
reviews.summary: Prune4Web turns DOM reduction for web agents into generated Python scoring programs, reducing candidate elements by 25-50x and raising low-level grounding accuracy from 46.80% to 88.28%.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Existing multimodal web-agent DOM processing compared with Prune4Web programmatic pruning.
Figure 2. Prune4Web pipeline with planner, programmatic element filter, and action grounder.
Figure 3. Recall@N and benchmark/ablation results for programmatic filtering.

tables.caption: Tables 1-5. Multimodal-Mind2Web performance, grounding accuracy, DOM filtering ablations, architecture ablation, and training-strategy ablation.

reviews.title: Less is More: Recursive Reasoning with Tiny Networks
reviews.summary: Tiny Recursive Model (TRM) replaces the complex hierarchical recursive reasoning model with a single 2-layer tiny network (7M params) that recursively refines its answer, achieving 45% on ARC-AGI-1 and 8% on ARC-AGI-2 — surpassing most LLMs including Deepseek R1, o3-mini, and Gemini 2.5 Pro with less than 0.01% of their parameters.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Tiny Recursion Model (TRM) recursively improves its answer with a tiny network.
Figure 2. Pseudocode of Hierarchical Reasoning Models (HRMs).
Figure 3. Pseudocode of Tiny Recursion Models (TRMs).

reviews.title: LitBench: A Benchmark and Dataset for Reliable Evaluation of Creative Writing
reviews.summary: A curated r/WritingPrompts benchmark and training corpus showing that trained reward models can evaluate creative writing more reliably than zero-shot LLM judges.

tables.caption: Table 1. LLM-as-a-judge evaluation results by model on LitBench.

reviews.title: How much do language models memorize?
reviews.summary: A formal information-theoretic decomposition of memorization into unintended memorization and generalization, measuring GPT-style model capacity at approximately 3.6 bits per parameter; training hundreds of transformers shows models memorize until capacity fills, at which point grokking begins and double descent occurs exactly when data size exceeds model capacity.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Unintended memorization of uniform random data. Memorization plateaus at the empirical capacity limit of GPT-family models, approximately 3.6 bits-per-parameter.
Figure 2. Unintended memorization of text across model and dataset sizes, calculated with respect to a large oracle model.
Figure 6. Capacity in bits-per-parameter for GPT models trained on synthetic data. Estimated α = 3.64 bits-per-parameter in half precision.

tables.caption: Table 1. Model capacity estimates across widths, depths, and precision (fp32 vs bf16).
Table 2. Model memorization across sequence length and vocabulary size.
Table 3. Scaling law coefficients for membership inference across model sizes.

reviews.title: WASP: Benchmarking Web Agent Security Against Prompt Injection Attacks
reviews.summary: A realistic benchmark for prompt injection attacks against web agents, showing high hijack rates but much lower full attacker goal completion.

figures.caption: Figure 1. WASP summary result and example injected webpage.
Figure 2. End-to-end attack example on a Reddit task.
Figure 3. Attack flow across first action, intermediate steps, and final state.

tables.caption: Table 1. Comparison with related security benchmarks.
Table 2. Main benchmark results.
Tables 3 and 4. Injection ablations.

reviews.title: RAG Without the Lag: Interactive Debugging for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Pipelines
reviews.summary: An interactive RAG debugging tool that wraps pipeline steps as Python primitives, auto-generates a browser UI, and uses precomputed indexes plus checkpoints for low-latency what-if iteration.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Example debugging flow in RAGGY: adjust chunking, add query rewriting, and observe the final answer improve.
Figure 2. Python RAG pipeline code and the generated debugging interface with Query, Retriever, LLM, and Answer cells.
Figure 3. Retriever cell controls for hyperparameters, manual chunk selection, and similarity-score visualization.

tables.caption: Table 1. User-study participants across software, ML, research, and leadership roles building production RAG applications.
Table 2. Common RAG failure modes and participant debugging tactics.

reviews.title: AgentRewardBench: Evaluating Automatic Evaluations of Web Agent Trajectories
reviews.summary: A benchmark of expert-labeled web-agent trajectories showing that current LLM judges have limited precision while rule-based evaluators often undercount valid successes.

figures.caption: Figure 1. AgentRewardBench example: an LLM judge evaluates a VisualWebArena trajectory against expert annotations.
Figure 2. AgentRewardBench creation process: collect trajectories, expert annotations, and judge evaluations.
Figure 3. Example judge error where an agent adds cereal to the cart but does not complete the requested purchase.

tables.caption: Table 1. Judge precision, recall, and F1 for predicting trajectory success.
Table 2. Input-representation ablation for the GPT-4o mini simplified judge.
Table 3. Expert, LLM-judge, and rule-based success-rate estimates across web-agent benchmarks.

reviews.title: Plan-and-Act: Improving Planning of Agents for Long-Horizon Tasks
reviews.summary: A planner/executor web-agent framework that trains explicit planning with grounded synthetic data, dynamic replanning, and CoT traces for stronger long-horizon web navigation.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Plan-and-Act planner/executor architecture.
Figure 2. Dynamic planner/executor loop for a GitHub web-navigation task.

reviews.title: WritingBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Generative Writing
reviews.summary: A 1,000-query benchmark and query-dependent critic framework for evaluating LLMs on realistic generative writing across domains, languages, source materials, and style, format, and length constraints.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Query example with color-coded requirements and linked writing support materials.

reviews.title: Cocoa: Co-Planning and Co-Execution with AI Agents
reviews.summary: An HCI system for document-based human-agent collaboration that exposes editable shared plans, assignable user/agent steps, stepwise execution, and replanning, improving steerability over chat without reducing ease of use.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Cocoa integrates interactive co-planning and co-execution into a document environment.
Figure 2. Cocoa user interface with assignable plan steps and editable agent outputs.
Figure 9. Lab-study ratings for steerability, ease of use, and general utility.

reviews.title: Large Language Models Empowered Personalized Web Agents
reviews.summary: A benchmark and memory-alignment framework for personalized web agents that use profiles and behavior histories to choose web functions and parameters.

reviews.title: AgentOccam: A Simple Yet Strong Baseline for LLM-Based Web Agents
reviews.summary: A minimalist web-agent baseline showing that LLM performance can improve sharply by aligning browser observation and action spaces instead of adding complex agent scaffolding.

figures.caption: Figure 1. AgentOccam overview: aligning action and observation spaces instead of adding compound LLM policies.
Figure 2. Action-space alignment: remove non-essential actions, disable scrolling, and add branch/prune planning.
Figure 4. Observation-space alignment: compact page elements, pivotal-node replay, and plan-scoped history.

tables.caption: Table 1. Component comparison across LLM-based web agents.

reviews.title: AssistantBench: Can Web Agents Solve Realistic and Time-Consuming Tasks?
reviews.summary: A realistic open-web benchmark of 214 time-consuming tasks showing that current web agents, retrieval systems, and closed-book models remain far from reliable.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Example AssistantBench task and why closed-book or retrieval-only systems struggle.

reviews.title: EQ-Bench: An Emotional Intelligence Benchmark for Large Language Models
reviews.summary: A 60-question benchmark for evaluating LLM emotional understanding through dialogue-based emotion-intensity ratings, reporting broad model separation, repeatability, and strong correlations with MMLU and Chatbot Arena.

reviews.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants
reviews.summary: A 466-question benchmark for general AI assistants that tests real-world tool use, web browsing, multimodal/file handling, and exact-answer robustness, revealing a large gap between humans and tool-augmented GPT-4.

reviews.title: Clarify When Necessary: Resolving Ambiguity Through Interaction with LMs
reviews.summary: A task-agnostic framework for resolving ambiguity through clarifying questions, decomposed into when to clarify, what to ask, and how to respond. INTENT-SIM estimates entropy over simulated user intents to identify ambiguous inputs, doubling random-selection gains at 10% interaction budget across QA, MT, and NLI.

figures.caption: Figure 1. Three-stage framework for resolving ambiguity with clarification questions.

tables.caption: Table 2. Performance on responsiveness to clarification. Follow = clarifying QA pair, Disambig = disambiguated input, Direct = baseline.
Table 4. Results for determining when to clarify. AUROC and performance under fixed interaction budgets.
Table 1. Example instances for each task for different ambiguity types.

reviews.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
reviews.summary: Language models exhibit a U-shaped performance curve when relevant information appears at different positions in long contexts — best at the beginning or end, worst in the middle. This holds across multi-document QA and key-value retrieval, even for long-context models.

figures.caption: Figure 1. U-shaped performance curve — changing the position of relevant information results in highest performance at the start and end, with significant degradation in the middle.
Figure 2. Multi-document QA performance across models and positions.
Figure 3. Key-value retrieval performance across context lengths and positions.

tables.caption: Table 1. Multi-document QA performance with 10, 20, and 30 total documents.
Table 2. Model performance statistics continued.
Table 3. Key-value retrieval performance results.
